title: The bar exam and the legends of the bar
primary_topics: legends of the bar (Diokno, Salonga, Teehankee)
sub_topics: 1960 Bar comparison (4,178 took, 1,667 passed, four Sundays at UE)
1999 attempt to lower passing grade from 75% to 74% (CJ objection)
Bar exam chair undisclosed nephew episode (reprimand + half honorarium)
2021 digitized and regionalized Bar exam (11,402 took, 8,241 passed, 72.28% average)
11 of 27 CJs (excluding Sereno) as bar placers
Maria Lourdes Sereno quo warranto removal
the doors of the legal profession 'must swing on reluctant hinges'

stances.claim: The doors of the legal profession must swing on 'reluctant hinges' — even during the COVID-19 emergency, the Supreme Court should be wary of wholesale entry; lawyering is invested in the public interest and lawyers are disciplined as public officers.

He disagrees with the non-disclosure of the top 10 bar placers — though topping the bar is neither an assurance of success nor a guarantee of superlative legal know-how, top 10 passers historically become great practitioners, professors, corporate counsels, and government officials, sought-after by top firms and paid the highest compensation possible.

At least 11 of 27 CJs (excluding Sereno who 'was ousted via quo warranto and deemed never to have been a de jure chief justice') are bar placers: Yulo (3rd), Paras (2nd), Bengzon (2nd), Concepcion (1st), Makalintal (7th), Aquino (9th), Teehankee (1st), Yap (1st), Narvasa (2nd), Panganiban (6th), Bersamin (9th).

Claudio Teehankee is the greatest Ateneo law alumnus of all time — summa cum laude AB and LLB, numero uno in the 1940 Bar; though he owed his appointment as DOJ secretary and SC justice to Marcos Sr., he remained independent and wrote (or concurred with) decisions unfavorable to him.
stances.rhetorical_move: independence-despite-appointment praise + 'in my humble view' superlative

institutions: Panganiban, Parlade, Benitez, and Africa (his law firm)

signature_phrases: the first key to the justice system
the penumbra of their great shadows
the greatest Ateneo law alumnus of all time
Please prepare our trial brief extra well
rules should be obeyed and should not be changed midway
register_markers: AFTER ALL, OUR PROFESSION IS THE FIRST KEY
self-quotation of his bar-legends set piece (verbatim from C05)
decision_framework_signals: applies 'reluctant hinges' framework to professional gatekeeping — quality over quantity
uses historical-empirical evidence (11/27 CJs as bar placers) to justify retaining top-10 disclosure practice
frames Sereno's quo warranto removal as making her 'never a de jure chief justice' — exclusionary deemed-never doctrine
uses independence-despite-appointment principle (Teehankee under Marcos) as judicial-ethics standard
notable_anecdotes: His 1999 strong objection (as an incumbent associate justice) to lowering the bar passing grade from 75% to 74% — argument: 'rules should be obeyed and should not be changed midway'

1999 Bar exam chair (an associate justice) reprimanded by the Court and his honorarium slashed by half for failing to disclose that a nephew took the exam — which would have disqualified him from the chair
2021 first time top 10 passers were not named (now abandoned)
His Salonga-Diokno-Teehankee bar-legends set piece (verbatim from earlier columns)

Dissolving his law firm Panganiban, Parlade, Benitez, and Africa in emulation of Salonga's 1986 PCGG-occasioned disbandment — 'orphaning' partners Benitez (1959 1st), Parlade (1959 4th), and classmate Africa (1960 11th)
